Query: All are features of infant born to diabetic mother except:

Options:
A: Obesity
B: Learning disability
C: Ketotic hypogylycemia
D: Future diabetes mellitus

Document [0] (Title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Treatment / Management -- Management of MODY in the Youth) As mentioned above, GCK MODY generally does not require treatment except during pregnancy. Determining which GCK MODY patients need treatment is determined by the fetal genotype. This is not genetically determined but assessed by the abdominal circumference of the fetus measured during the second trimester. Should this value exceed 75%, this infers that the fetus does not carry the GCK mutation, and thus these mothers should be treated with insulin during pregnancy to help prevent macrosomia. If the fetus has an abdominal circumference less than 75%, this infers the fetus does not carry the gene as they will have the same glucose setpoint as the mother, which is increased, and thus maternal hyperglycemia will be sensed by the fetus as normal and therefore growth will be normal and maternal treatment with insulin is not indicated, and it can even result in growth restriction. [28]
Document [1] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Etiology) Additionally, clinicians should be aware of maturity-onset diabetes of the young, an autosomal dominant genetic condition that is often misdiagnosed as GDM. An estimated 5% of patients diagnosed with GDM have maturity-onset diabetes of the young. In patients with findings during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) of a fasting glucose of more than or equal to 5.5 mmol/L and a glucose increment from the fasting to 2-hour OGTT result of less than 4.6 mmol/L with a positive family history of diabetes, maturity-onset diabetes of the young should be suspected. Fasting glucose more than or equal to 100 mg/dL in patients with a body mass index (BMI) less than 25 kg/m 2 can also be used to help differentiate these conditions. [3] See StatPearls' companion reference, " Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young ," for more information on this condition.
Document [2] (Title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Deterrence and Patient Education) As discussed above, MODY is an autosomal dominant condition, and different genetic subtypes behave differently. Patient education is important in terms of teaching them that it runs in families, and thus genetic counseling becomes vital. Knowing that this condition runs in families can allow for adequate screening and implementation of treatment, if necessary, early to avoid complications. They must understand the disease and the need to follow up. Even though certain subtypes may only require dietary intervention, the disease may progress to the point of necessitating pharmacologic intervention. Certain subtypes have a higher propensity of developing complications, and therefore counseling patients on regular appropriate screenings and subspecialist follow-up is important. Women with MODY and the GCK mutation need to be made aware that they may require insulin treatment during pregnancy depending on the fetus genotype, and they should be monitored closely by a specialist during their pregnancies.

Document [4] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction) Since then, the evolution of diagnostic criteria for GDM has reflected ongoing scientific inquiry and shifting standards in maternal-fetal medicine. From the adaptation of glucose thresholds by international bodies like the World Health Organization (WHO) to the endorsement of comprehensive criteria by the International Association of the Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups (IADPSG) in 2013, GDM management approaches continue to evolve. These advancements highlight not only the complexity of diagnosing GDM but also underscore the critical need for evidence-based guidelines that mitigate risks for both mothers and infants. As diagnostic strategies continue to refine, ongoing research aims to optimize maternal and perinatal outcomes while addressing the diverse healthcare needs of pregnant women globally. [1] [3]

Document [6] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Differential Diagnosis) Many women do not receive the appropriate screening for diabetes mellitus before pregnancy, so in some cases, it is challenging to distinguish GDM from preexisting diabetes and maturity-onset diabetes of the young. [18] [19]
Document [7] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Prognosis) Proper control of maternal glucose levels significantly reduces GDM risks, including macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycemia. The risk of preeclampsia decreases from 18% to 12% with treatment. [1] If GDM interventions, including dietary modifications or pharmacologic therapy, are implemented early enough, progression to type 2 diabetes in 10 years is reduced by 35% to 40%. Furthermore, even small reductions in BMI can result in a reduced risk for diabetes by 25%. [20]

Document [9] (Title: Macrosomia -- Prognosis) A patient who delivers a macrosomic infant should be screened very carefully for previously undiagnosed diabetes. If such screening is negative, they should be monitored carefully in subsequent pregnancies. The goal of scheduled cesarean birth for suspected macrosomia is to reduce fetal morbidity or maternal morbidity, or both. Although fetal and maternal morbidity increases with birth weights >4,000 g, most births of macrosomic newborns are uncomplicated. [10]
Document [10] (Title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Etiology -- Monogenic Diabetes) A single genetic mutation in an autosomal dominant gene causes this type of diabetes. Examples of monogenic diabetes include conditions like neonatal diabetes mellitus and maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY). Around 1 to 5% of all diabetes cases are due to monogenic diabetes. MODY is a familial disorder and usually presents under the age of 25 years.
Document [11] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction) Over several decades, GDM management approaches have continued to evolve due to alarmingly high perinatal mortality rates among women with diabetes during pregnancy. A significant leap came in 1964 with the publication of O’Sullivan and Mahan’s landmark study that defined specific diagnostic criteria for GDM in the United States. Their criteria, based on a 100-gram 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test, were designed to identify women at increased risk, aiming to correlate maternal hyperglycemia with adverse perinatal outcomes. Subsequent validation studies underscored the importance of these thresholds in predicting both immediate complications and long-term health risks for mothers and their children. [1] [3]
Document [12] (Title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Clinical Significance) There is compelling evidence that early treatment of diabetes can reduce microvascular complications of diabetes. [15] The prompt treatment of the disease has significantly improved mortality, especially related to vascular disease. [5] The evidence for the impact of screening for diabetes is increasing. It is considered to be 1 of the more straightforward ways to prevent and reduce morbidity and mortality of this disease. Excess mortality related to type 2 diabetes results from cardiovascular events, and early screening has the potential to allow for early intervention. The UKPDS demonstrated a 14% reduction in myocardial infarctions for every 1% decrease in HbA1c value. [16] Gestational diabetes is an identified cause of several adverse outcomes during pregnancy, like pre-eclampsia, macrosomia, birth trauma, perinatal mortality, and neonatal cardiac, respiratory, and metabolic complications. [17] There is also a long-term risk of developing type 1/type 2 diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular disease in the mother, as well as metabolic syndrome, obesity, hypertension, and impaired glucose tolerance in the child. Identifying this early and prompt management has a positive impact on the health of the mother as well as the child. [18]

Document [14] (Title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) MODY is caused by defects in pancreatic islet cell development and insulin secretion. It is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion, and the patients generally have heterozygote mutations. Penetrance and expressivity can vary immensely, even among family members. MODY genes affect insulin secretion via impairment of insulin sensing, glucose metabolism in beta cells, or activation of adenosine triphosphate (ATP)-dependent potassium channels. The disorder is best managed by an interprofessional team that consists of a diabetic nurse, endocrinologist, internist, geneticist, dietitian, ophthalmologist, nephrologist, occasionally gynecologist (MODY 5), and a cardiologist. These patients are prone to the same complications as other diabetics except for patients with the GCK mutation, and early referral to a specialist is highly recommended. [Level 5]
Document [15] (Title: Diabetes -- Introduction) Diabetes mellitus (DM) is a metabolic disease, involving inappropriately elevated blood glucose levels. DM has several categories, including type 1, type 2, maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY), gestational diabetes, neonatal diabetes, and secondary causes due to endocrinopathies, steroid use, etc. The main subtypes of DM are Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) and Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), which classically result from defective insulin secretion (T1DM) and/or action (T2DM). T1DM presents in children or adolescents, while T2DM is thought to affect middle-aged and older adults who have prolonged hyperglycemia due to poor lifestyle and dietary choices. The pathogenesis for T1DM and T2DM is drastically different, and therefore each type has various etiologies, presentations, and treatments.
Document [16] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Continuing Education Activity) Objectives: Identify risk factors for gestational diabetes mellitus to facilitate early recognition and intervention. Differentiate between gestational diabetes mellitus and other types of diabetes to ensure accurate diagnosis and appropriate management. Select appropriate therapeutic interventions for treating gestational diabetes mellitus based on individual patient needs and evolving best practices. Collaborate with an interprofessional team to provide holistic care for patients with gestational diabetes mellitus and coordinate follow-up care to ensure ongoing management and prevent long-term complications. Document [21] (Title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Etiology -- Gestational Diabetes Mellitus) GDM can be complicated by hypertension, preeclampsia, and hydramnios and may also lead to increased operative interventions. The fetus can have increased weight and size (macrosomia) or congenital anomalies. Even after birth, such infants may have respiratory distress syndrome and subsequent childhood and adolescent obesity. Older age, obesity, excessive gestational weight gain, history of congenital anomalies in previous children, or stillbirth, or a family history of diabetes are risk factors for GDM.

Document [24] (Title: Diabetes -- Complications) Those with gestational diabetes are at a higher risk for cesarean delivery and chronic hypertension. Pregnant patients with T2DM generally have a better prognosis in terms of neonatal and pregnancy complications compared to those with T1DM. Generally, neonates of DM mothers will present with hypoglycemia and macrosomia. [52]
Document [25] (Title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction) Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is typically defined as hyperglycemia that is diagnosed or develops during pregnancy. GDM is often divided into classes, primarily diet-controlled GDM (class A1GDM) or GDM requiring pharmacologic treatment of hyperglycemia (class A2GDM). [1] The benefits of identifying GDM have long been established, with several studies demonstrating that women diagnosed with GDM are at high risk for developing type 2 diabetes long-term and that antepartum GDM treatment reduces adverse pregnancy outcomes. However, a consensus on the diagnostic criteria for GDM or the optimal timing for pregnancy screening has not been reached. [2]
Document [26] (Title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Treatment / Management) Nutritional interventions are a key component of care and are typically the first recommendation for pregnant women with diabetes. Dietary interventions should be individualized to help achieve glycemic targets and support appropriate gestational weight gain. The recommended carbohydrate intake during pregnancy is 175 g/d. Emphasizing high-quality carbohydrates can lead to improved fasting and postprandial glucose control. [13] In addition, the American Association of Clinical Endocrinology (AACE) recommends small, frequent meals, including a bedtime snack, to help prevent overnight hypoglycemia and ketosis.
Document [27] (Title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Etiology) DM is broadly classified into three types by etiology and clinical presentation, type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and gestational diabetes (GDM). Some other less common types of diabetes include monogenic diabetes and secondary diabetes. [2] [3] [4] [5]
Document [28] (Title: Beckwith-Wiedemann Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis) Neonatal macrosomia, macroglossia, and hypoglycemia should prompt a comprehensive evaluation for other diagnoses, such as maternal diabetes mellitus. Other causes of developmental delay must be considered in children with developmental delay but without chromosomal abnormalities or a history of prematurity, birth trauma, or neonatal hypoglycemia. [19]
Document [29] (Title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Gestational Diabetes) Gestational diabetes adversely impacts the lives of both fetus and mother, making screening for it an essential part of obstetric care. Screening is necessary for 1 of the risk factors for diabetes. In addition to the risk factors discussed above, others include: History of prediabetes (impaired glucose tolerance/fasting glucose, HbA1C>5.7) Prior history of gestational diabetes BMI>30 kg/m 2 Family history of diabetes Multiple gestations Old maternal age Glycosuria at the first prenatal visit History of unexplained miscarriage or malformation

Document [31] (Title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- History and Physical) History should first include a thorough personal medical history. The diabetes diagnosis characteristic of MODY occurs in adolescence or early adulthood. Therefore details surrounding their diabetes diagnosis become of utmost importance. Also, birth history can provide some helpful information if known. Intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) is seen in those with HNF1B (MODY 5) in addition to some congenital abnormalities such as renal and urogenital tract anomalies as well as pancreatic hypoplasia. Those with the HNF4A (MODY 1) subtype may have a history of a birth weight generally more than 800gr above normal, as well as transient neonatal hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. GCK (MODY 2) patients may have had a history of mild fasting hyperglycemia at birth. Finally, some of those with HFN1A (MODY 3) may have had transient neonatal hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. [17] A further personal medical history should include a full review of systems and enquire about known medical problems involving other organ systems as some forms of MODY can be associated with extra-beta cell manifestations, including renal, hepatic, genitourinary, exocrine pancreatic, or intestinal effects.

Diabetes mellitus also underlies glucose intolerance that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, as reflected by an inverse‑isa relationship with “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium.” Certain drugs, such as the progestin desogestrel and the agent ethynodioldiacetát, are contraindicated in patients with diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Clinically, diabetes mellitus is associated with several serious complications.
Document [18] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181) Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is classified under the broader category Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, indicating that it is a subset of the clinical indications guiding obstetric management. Within this group of obstetric complications, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication, highlighting that gestational diabetes and related glucose intolerance are important conditions to consider when evaluating and treating patients during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period.

Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 671) Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that connects a wide array of metabolic, developmental, obstetric, ophthalmic, musculoskeletal, infectious and pharmacologic concepts. All recognized forms of neonatal diabetes—including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal 1, and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are mapped to مرض السكري, underscoring its relevance in early‑life glucose dysregulation. Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683) Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2495) Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is linked to a range of clinical manifestations and therapeutic considerations. Toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية) are clinically associated with diabetes, reflecting the disease’s potential to cause severe peripheral vascular and ocular complications. Specific forms of diabetes that occur in the neonatal period, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder)), are mapped to the broader category of diabetes, indicating that these transient neonatal presentations are recognized as variants of the disease. Additionally, the combination of developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes is also mapped to diabetes, highlighting a possible neurodevelopmental syndrome associated with early‑onset diabetes. From a pharmacologic perspective, the hormonal contraceptives desogestrel and norgestrel are contraindicated in individuals with diabetes, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in diabetic patients. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse systemic effects of diabetes, its neonatal forms, associated neurological conditions, and important medication contraindications that are essential for comprehensive patient care.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841) DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the disease مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal forms of diabetes and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are considered manifestations of diabetes. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting the well‑known link between diabetes and retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy. In addition, malfunction of the human INSR protein is gene‑product‑associated with مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Finally, متلازمة الأيض is described as a child condition of مرض السكري, underscoring the close relationship between diabetes and metabolic syndrome in patient care and disease management.

Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2626) Secondary diabetes mellitus, without mention of complication is classified as a subtype of diabetes mellitus without complication, indicating that it is a secondary form of diabetes that does not have documented complications and is considered a child entity under the broader parent category of diabetes mellitus without complication.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2509) Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are collectively mapped to the neonatal disorder described by the Arabic term اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, indicating that this combination of neurodevelopmental delay, seizure activity, and early‑onset diabetes is recognized as a specific disorder of the newborn. Likewise, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1—referring to transient neonatal diabetes mellitus—is also mapped to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, showing that this temporary form of diabetes in the newborn falls under the same category of neonatal disorder. Together, these mappings underscore that both the triad of developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes and transient neonatal diabetes mellitus represent manifestations of a disorder occurring in the newborn period, emphasizing the importance of early identification and integrated management in neonatal care.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479) Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434) Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a broad category that encompasses specific maternal health problems such as hypertension and/or vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance occurring during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. Both hypertension‑related vomiting and gestational diabetes represent distinct subtypes of the overall complications associated with the perinatal period, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and targeted management of these conditions to protect maternal and fetal outcomes.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 535) Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is presented as an overarching disorder that includes several specific entities: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder); Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1; and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, all of which are mapped to مرض السكري. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is identified as a particular manifestation of diabetes, indicating that gestational or pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance falls under the same disease umbrella. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders that are not themselves diabetes, highlighting a broader involvement of pancreatic pathology. It is also linked to retinal abnormalities, specifically اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the eye complications commonly seen in diabetic patients. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is described as a child of مرض السكري, suggesting that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream or component condition of diabetes. Both مرض السكري and متلازمة الأيض share the GDC value “komorbidita,” reflecting their status as comorbid conditions. An additional entry labeled “Unknown” also carries the komorbidita value, indicating the presence of an unspecified comorbidity related to the same category. Overall, the knowledge base connects various neonatal and pregnancy‑related forms of diabetes, associated pancreatic and retinal disorders, and the comorbid relationship with metabolic syndrome, emphasizing the multifaceted nature of diabetes mellitus and its relevance for comprehensive clinical assessment and management.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460) Diabetes (مرض السكري) includes specific forms such as DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), which is mapped to the broader category of diabetes, and the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium,” which is an inverse subclass of diabetes. Clinically, diabetes is associated with retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية), highlighting its potential impact on ocular health. Moreover, diabetes is linked to various risk factors (rizikové faktory), emphasizing the need to identify and manage these contributors to improve patient outcomes.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1699) DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all specific forms or presentations that map to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes). The hormonal contraceptive desogestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that use of this medication should be avoided in diabetic individuals. Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a distinct clinical scenario that falls under مرض السكري, highlighting the importance of careful management of glucose levels during the perinatal period. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Additionally, the term rizikové faktory (risk factors) is linked to مرض السكري, underscoring that various risk factors contribute to the development and progression of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse manifestations, comorbidities, contraindications, and risk considerations associated with مرض السكري.
